studies.title: Conservative treatment of fecal incontinence: Triple target treatment versus low frequent electrostimulation
studies.health_condition:
Free text:
Fecal incontinence, grade I and above
ICD-10-GM (translation):
R15 - Faecal incontinence
Healthy volunteers:
No Entry
studies.brief_summary:
In order to treat fecal incontinence, various surgical and conservative
options are currently available. Conservative treatments like physiotherapy, biofeedback and electrical stimulation target to improve strength and coordination of the sphincter muscles. Due to anatomical reasons it is not possible to strengthen all import structures of the pelvic floor and the sphincters merely by gymnastic exercises. The internal sphincter is constructed by smooth muscle cells, which are not consciously accessible. But also the other important parts up to 75% consist of slow muscle fibers,
which are difficult to train by physical exercises. Smooth muscles and slow fibers however positively react on electrical currents so that stimulation therapy is recommended in fecal incontinence.
Electrical Stimulation conducts small currents via electrodes into the human body, where they mimic natural electrical functions. For example a pace maker triggers heart beats, once the natural impulses are too weak or
studies.intervention:
Arm 1:
Contrain (Procon GmbH, 22393 Hamburg, Germany) will be used with rectal sitting electrode. The combination EMG biofeedback plus electrostimulation will be used. Patients will be treated with a carrier wave of 25 KHz and biphase modulation of impulse chains of 40Hz. Anxious patients will train in the biofeedback mode only for the first four weeks. Stimulation will be added after four weeks. Patients should train at home with an alternating conbination in the morning and with EMG triggered

classification.reason: The study does not explicitly mention randomization, controlled comparison, or prospective design. While it discusses treatment options, there is no clear indication of an interventional randomized trial. The lack of specific RCT-related keywords like 'randomized' or 'placebo-controlled' leans towards an observational or real-world study.
